mn119:1.2: at one time, the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn119:10.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* might see a corpse discarded in a charnel ground, being devoured by crows, or being devoured by hawks, or being devoured by vultures, or being devoured by herons, or being devoured by dogs, or being devoured by tigers, or being devoured by leopards, or being devoured by jackals, or being devoured by various kinds of creatures.
mn119:10.2: He compares this very body with it:
mn119:10.3: ‘This body too is of such a nature, will become such, and has not gone beyond this.’
mn119:10.5: in this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* develops [*bhāveti*] body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:11-14.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* might see a corpse discarded in a charnel ground, a skeleton with flesh and blood, held together by sinews …
mn119:11-14.2: a skeleton without flesh but smeared with blood, held together by sinews …
mn119:11-14.3: a skeleton with flesh and blood gone, held together by sinews …
mn119:11-14.4: bones disconnected and scattered in every direction: here a hand bone, there a foot bone, there an ankle bone, there a shin bone, there a thigh bone, there a hip bone, there a rib bone, there a spine bone, there a shoulder bone, there a neck bone, there a jaw bone, there a tooth bone, there the skull.
mn119:11-14.5: He compares this very body with it:
mn119:11-14.6: ‘This body too is of such a nature, will become such, and has not gone beyond this.’
mn119:11-14.8: in this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* develops [*bhāveti*] body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:15-17.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* might see a corpse discarded in a charnel ground—
mn119:15-17.2: bones white, the color of shells …
mn119:15-17.3: bones piled up, more than a year old …
mn119:15-17.4: bones rotten and crumbled to powder.
mn119:15-17.5: He compares this very body with it:
mn119:15-17.6: ‘This body too is of such a nature, will become such, and has not gone beyond this.’
mn119:15-17.9: in this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* develops [*bhāveti*] body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:18.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, quite secluded [*vivicceva*] from sensual pleasures … enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*.
mn119:18.2: He drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajena*], so that there is no part of his whole body unpervaded by *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajena*].
mn119:18.3: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, a skilled bath attendant or bath attendant’s apprentice were to pour bath powder into a bronze basin and knead it, sprinkling it again and again with water, so that that ball of bath powder would be permeated with moisture, suffused with moisture, pervaded inside and out by moisture, yet would not drip.
mn119:18.4: So too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajena*], so that there is no part of his whole body unpervaded by *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajena*].
mn119:18.7: in this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* develops [*bhāveti*] body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:19.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, with the stilling of thoughts [*vitakka*] and examinations [*vicāra*] … enters and dwells in the second *jhāna*.
mn119:19.2: He drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi*, so that there is no part of his whole body unpervaded by *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi*.
mn119:19.3: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, there were a deep lake fed by springs. It would have no inflow of water from the eastern direction, no inflow of water from the western direction, no inflow of water from the northern direction, no inflow of water from the southern direction; nor would the sky send down showers from time to time. But cool streams of water would well up from within that very lake, drenching, steeping, filling, and pervading that very lake with cool water, so that there would be no part of the whole lake unpervaded by cool water.
mn119:19.4: So too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi*, so that there is no part of his whole body unpervaded by *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi*.
mn119:19.6: in this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* develops [*bhāveti*] body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:2.1: Then, when several *bhikkhus*, after the meal, returned from almsround, were sitting together and gathered in the attendance hall, this discussion arose among them:
mn119:2.10: This, *bhante*, was our discussion left unfinished when the *Bhagavā* arrived.”
mn119:2.3: How the *Bhagavā*, knowing and seeing, the *arahant*, the fully awakened one, has said that body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsati*], when developed [*bhāvitā*] and cultivated, is of great fruit and great benefit.”
mn119:2.4: And this discussion among those *bhikkhus* was left unfinished. Then, in the evening, the *Bhagavā* rose from seclusion [*paṭisallānā*] and went to the attendance hall; having gone there, he sat down on the seat prepared.
mn119:2.6: “What discussion, *bhikkhus*, are you sitting together for now? And what was your discussion left unfinished?”
mn119:2.7: “Here, *bhante*, when we, after the meal, returned from almsround, were sitting together and gathered in the attendance hall, this discussion arose among us:
mn119:2.9: How the *Bhagavā*, knowing and seeing, the *arahant*, the fully awakened one, has said that body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsati*], when developed [*bhāvitā*] and cultivated, is of great fruit and great benefit.’
mn119:20.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, with the fading away of *pīti* … enters and dwells in the third *jhāna*.
mn119:20.2: He drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with *sukha* free from *pīti*, so that there is no part of his whole body unpervaded by *sukha* free from *pīti*.
mn119:20.3: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, in a pond of blue lotuses, red lotuses, or white lotuses, some blue lotuses, red lotuses, or white lotuses are born in the water, grow in the water, do not rise above the water, and are nourished immersed within it. From their tips down to their roots, they are drenched, steeped, filled, and pervaded with cool water, so that there would be no part of those whole blue lotuses, red lotuses, or white lotuses unpervaded by cool water.
mn119:20.4: So too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with *sukha* free from *pīti*, so that there is no part of his whole body unpervaded by *sukha* free from *pīti*.
mn119:20.6: in this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* develops [*bhāveti*] body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:21.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, with the abandoning of *sukha* … enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*.
mn119:21.2: He sits pervading this very body with a pure and bright mind [*cetasā*], so that there is no part of his whole body unpervaded by a pure and bright mind [*cetasā*].
mn119:21.3: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, a man were sitting wrapped from head to foot in a white cloth, so that there would be no part of his whole body unpervaded by the white cloth.
mn119:21.4: So too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* sits pervading this very body with a pure and bright mind [*cetasā*], so that there is no part of his whole body unpervaded by a pure and bright mind [*cetasā*].
mn119:21.5: As he dwells thus diligent, ardent, and resolute, memories and intentions connected with the household life are abandoned.
mn119:21.6: With their abandonment, internally his mind [*cittaṁ*] stands still, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi*], and enters *samādhi*.
mn119:21.7: In this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* develops [*bhāveti*] body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:22.1: For anyone, *bhikkhus*, whose body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed [*bhāvitā*] and cultivated, whatever wholesome *dhammas* are connected with knowledge are included within it.
mn119:22.2: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, someone had mentally pervaded the great ocean; whatever streams flow into the ocean would be included within that.
mn119:22.3: So too, *bhikkhus*, for anyone whose body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed [*bhāvitā*] and cultivated, whatever wholesome *dhammas* are connected with knowledge are included within it.
mn119:23.1: For anyone, *bhikkhus*, whose body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsati*] has not been developed [*abhāvitā*] and not cultivated, Māra finds an opening in them, Māra finds a basis in them.
mn119:23.2: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, a man were to throw a heavy stone ball into a heap of wet clay.
mn119:23.4: Would that heavy stone ball find an opening in the heap of wet clay?”
mn119:23.6: “So too, *bhikkhus*, for anyone whose body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsati*] has not been developed [*abhāvitā*] and not cultivated, Māra finds an opening in them, Māra finds a basis in them.
mn119:24.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, there were dry, withered wood.
mn119:24.2: Then a man came with an upper fire-stick, thinking:
mn119:24.3: ‘I will produce fire; I will make heat appear.’
mn119:24.5: Would that man, rubbing that dry, withered wood with the upper fire-stick, produce fire and make heat appear?”
mn119:24.7: “So too, *bhikkhus*, for anyone whose body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsati*] has not been developed [*abhāvitā*] and not cultivated, Māra finds an opening in them, Māra finds a basis in them.
mn119:25.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, there were an empty, hollow water jar set on a stand.
mn119:25.2: Then a man came carrying a load of water.
mn119:25.4: Would that man find a place to deposit the water?”
mn119:25.6: “So too, *bhikkhus*, for anyone whose body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsati*] has not been developed [*abhāvitā*] and not cultivated, Māra finds an opening in them, Māra finds a basis in them.
mn119:26.1: For anyone, *bhikkhus*, whose body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed [*bhāvitā*] and cultivated, Māra does not find an opening in them, Māra does not find a basis in them.
mn119:26.2: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, a man were to throw a light ball of thread at a door panel made entirely of heartwood.
mn119:26.4: Would that man find an opening for that light ball of thread in the door panel made entirely of heartwood?”
mn119:26.6: “So too, *bhikkhus*, for anyone whose body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed [*bhāvitā*] and cultivated, Māra does not find an opening in them, Māra does not find a basis in them.
mn119:27.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, there were wet, sappy wood.
mn119:27.2: Then a man came with an upper fire-stick, thinking:
mn119:27.3: ‘I will produce fire; I will make heat appear.’
mn119:27.5: Would that man, rubbing that wet, sappy wood with the upper fire-stick, produce fire and make heat appear?”
mn119:27.7: “So too, *bhikkhus*, for anyone whose body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed [*bhāvitā*] and cultivated, Māra does not find an opening in them, Māra does not find a basis in them.
mn119:28.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, there were a water jar full of water, filled to the brim so a crow could drink from it, set on a stand.
mn119:28.2: Then a man came carrying a load of water.
mn119:28.4: Would that man find a place to deposit the water?”
mn119:28.6: “So too, *bhikkhus*, for anyone whose body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed [*bhāvitā*] and cultivated, Māra does not find an opening in them, Māra does not find a basis in them.
mn119:29.1: For anyone, *bhikkhus*, whose body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed [*bhāvitā*] and cultivated, whichever *dhamma* realizable by direct knowledge he inclines his mind [*cittaṁ*] toward for realization by direct knowledge, he attains the capacity to witness it then and there, whenever there is a suitable dimension [*āyatane*].
mn119:29.2: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, there were a water jar full of water, filled to the brim so a crow could drink from it, set on a stand.
mn119:29.3: If a strong man tipped it in whatever direction, would water come out?”
mn119:29.5: “So too, *bhikkhus*, for anyone whose body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed [*bhāvitā*] and cultivated, whichever *dhamma* realizable by direct knowledge he inclines his mind [*cittaṁ*] toward for realization by direct knowledge, he attains the capacity to witness it then and there, whenever there is a suitable dimension [*āyatane*].
mn119:3.1: “And how, *bhikkhus*, is body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsati*] developed [*bhāvitā*] and cultivated so that it is of great fruit and great benefit?
mn119:30.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, on level ground there were a square lotus pond, embanked, full of water, filled to the brim so a crow could drink from it.
mn119:30.2: If a strong man opened the embankment in whatever direction, would water come out?”
mn119:30.4: “So too, *bhikkhus*, for anyone whose body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed [*bhāvitā*] and cultivated, whichever *dhamma* realizable by direct knowledge he inclines his mind [*cittaṁ*] toward for realization by direct knowledge, he attains the capacity to witness it then and there, whenever there is a suitable dimension [*āyatane*].
mn119:31.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, on good ground at a crossroads there stood a fine thoroughbred chariot, harnessed, with the goad laid ready.
mn119:31.2: A skilled trainer, a driver of horses to be tamed, would mount it, take the reins in his left hand and the goad in his right hand, and drive it forward and back wherever he wished, however he wished.
mn119:31.3: So too, *bhikkhus*, for anyone whose body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsati*] has been developed [*bhāvitā*] and cultivated, whichever *dhamma* realizable by direct knowledge he inclines his mind [*cittaṁ*] toward for realization by direct knowledge, he attains the capacity to witness it then and there, whenever there is a suitable dimension [*āyatane*].
mn119:32.1: When body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāya satiyā*] has been practiced, developed [*bhāvitāya*], cultivated, made a vehicle, made a basis, sustained, accumulated, and well undertaken, *bhikkhus*, ten benefits may be expected.
mn119:33.1: One becomes able to endure discontent and delight; discontent does not overcome one, and one dwells overcoming arisen discontent.
mn119:34.1: One becomes able to endure fear and dread; fear and dread do not overcome one, and one dwells overcoming arisen fear and dread.
mn119:35.1: One is able to endure cold and heat, hunger and thirst, the touch of flies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and creeping things, and abusive and unwelcome ways of speech; one becomes one who can bear arisen bodily *vedanās* that are painful, sharp, harsh, bitter, unpleasant, disagreeable, and life-threatening.
mn119:36.1: One obtains at will, without trouble or difficulty, the four *jhānas*, which belong to the higher mind and are pleasant dwellings in this very life.
mn119:37.1: One experiences many kinds of psychic power. Having been one, one becomes many; having been many, one becomes one; one appears and vanishes … even as far as the brahmā world, one exercises mastery with the body.
mn119:38.1: With the purified divine ear element surpassing the human, one hears both kinds of sounds, divine and human, whether far or near ….
mn119:39.1: One understands the minds [*ceto*] of other beings and other persons, having encompassed them with one’s own mind [*cetasā*].
mn119:39.17: an unliberated mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘an unliberated mind [*cittaṁ*].’
mn119:39.2: One understands a mind [*cittaṁ*] with desire as ‘a mind [*cittaṁ*] with desire’;
mn119:4.1: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, gone to the wilderness, or gone to the root of a tree, or gone to an empty dwelling, sits down, folding his legs crosswise, setting his body upright, and establishing *sati* before him.
mn119:4.2: With *sati*, he breathes in; with *sati*, he breathes out.
mn119:4.3: Breathing in long, he understands, ‘I breathe in long’; or breathing out long, he understands, ‘I breathe out long.’
mn119:4.4: Breathing in short, he understands, ‘I breathe in short’; or breathing out short, he understands, ‘I breathe out short.’
mn119:4.5: He trains, ‘Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe in’; he trains, ‘Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe out.’
mn119:4.6: He trains, ‘Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra*, I will breathe in’; he trains, ‘Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra*, I will breathe out.’
mn119:4.7: As he dwells thus diligent, ardent, and resolute, memories and intentions connected with the household life are abandoned.
mn119:4.8: With their abandonment, internally his mind [*cittaṁ*] stands still, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi*], and enters *samādhi*.
mn119:4.9: In this way, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* develops [*bhāveti*] body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:40.1: One recollects many kinds of past lives, namely: one birth, two births … thus with their aspects and particulars one recollects many kinds of past lives.
mn119:41.1: With the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, one sees beings passing away and being reborn, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, well-gone and badly gone, and understands beings according to their *kamma*.
mn119:42.1: With the destruction of the *āsavas*, one realizes for oneself with direct knowledge in this very life the *āsava*-free liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*] and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*], and enters and dwells in it.
mn119:43.1: When body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāya satiyā*] has been practiced, developed [*bhāvitāya*], cultivated, made a vehicle, made a basis, sustained, accumulated, and well undertaken, *bhikkhus*, these ten benefits may be expected.”
mn119:43.3: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* rejoiced in what the *Bhagavā* had said.
mn119:43.4: The Sutta on Body-Directed *Sati* [*Kāyagatāsatisuttaṁ*] is finished, the ninth.
mn119:5.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, when walking, a *bhikkhu* understands, ‘I am walking’; when standing, he understands, ‘I am standing’; when sitting, he understands, ‘I am sitting’; when lying down, he understands, ‘I am lying down.’
mn119:5.2: In whatever way his body is positioned, he understands it in that way.
mn119:5.3: As he dwells thus diligent, ardent, and resolute, memories and intentions connected with the household life are abandoned.
mn119:5.4: With their abandonment, internally his mind [*cittaṁ*] stands still, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi*], and enters *samādhi*.
mn119:5.5: In this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* develops [*bhāveti*] body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:6.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when going forward and returning; acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when looking ahead and looking aside; acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when bending and stretching; acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when carrying the outer robe, bowl, and robe; acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when eating, drinking, chewing, and tasting; acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when defecating and urinating; acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when walking, standing, sitting, sleeping, waking, speaking, and keeping silent.
mn119:6.2: As he dwells thus diligent, ardent, and resolute, memories and intentions connected with the household life are abandoned.
mn119:6.3: With their abandonment, internally his mind [*cittaṁ*] stands still, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi*], and enters *samādhi*.
mn119:6.4: In this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* develops [*bhāveti*] body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:7.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* reflects upon this very body, upward from the soles of the feet and downward from the tips of the hair, bounded by skin, full of many kinds of impurity:
mn119:7.10: With their abandonment, internally his mind [*cittaṁ*] stands still, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi*], and enters *samādhi*.
mn119:7.11: In this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* develops [*bhāveti*] body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:7.2: ‘In this body there are head-hairs, body-hairs, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, pleura, spleen, lungs, intestines, mesentery, stomach contents, feces, bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, grease, saliva, mucus, synovial fluid, and urine.’
mn119:7.3: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, there were a bag with openings at both ends, full of various kinds of grain, namely—
mn119:7.4: hill rice, paddy rice, mung beans, peas, sesame seeds, and husked rice.
mn119:7.5: A person with good eyes would open it and reflect:
mn119:7.6: ‘These are hill rice, these are paddy rice, these are mung beans, these are peas, these are sesame seeds, these are husked rice.’
mn119:7.7: So too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* reflects upon this very body, upward from the soles of the feet and downward from the tips of the hair, bounded by skin, full of many kinds of impurity:
mn119:7.8: ‘In this body there are head-hairs, body-hairs, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, pleura, spleen, lungs, intestines, mesentery, stomach contents, feces, bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, grease, saliva, mucus, synovial fluid, and urine.’
mn119:7.9: As he dwells thus diligent, ardent, and resolute, memories and intentions connected with the household life are abandoned.
mn119:8.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* reflects upon this very body, however it is placed, however it is positioned, in terms of elements:
mn119:8.2: ‘In this body there are the earth element, the water element, the fire element, and the air element.’
mn119:8.3: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, a skilled cattle butcher or cattle butcher’s apprentice, having slaughtered a cow, were sitting at a crossroads after cutting it into pieces.
mn119:8.4: So too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* reflects upon this very body, however it is placed, however it is positioned, in terms of elements:
mn119:8.5: ‘In this body there are the earth element, the water element, the fire element, and the air element.’
mn119:8.6: As he dwells thus diligent, ardent, and resolute, memories and intentions connected with the household life are abandoned.
mn119:8.7: With their abandonment, internally his mind [*cittaṁ*] stands still, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi*], and enters *samādhi*.
mn119:8.8: In this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* develops [*bhāveti*] body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
mn119:9.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* might see a corpse discarded in a charnel ground, one day dead, two days dead, or three days dead, bloated, livid, and oozing matter.
mn119:9.2: He compares this very body with it:
mn119:9.3: ‘This body too is of such a nature, will become such, and has not gone beyond this.’
mn119:9.4: As he dwells thus diligent, ardent, and resolute, memories and intentions connected with the household life are abandoned.
mn119:9.5: With their abandonment, internally his mind [*cittaṁ*] stands still, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi*], and enters *samādhi*.
mn119:9.6: In this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* develops [*bhāveti*] body-directed *sati* [*kāyagatāsatiṁ*].
